factual-accuracy.requirement: States combined federal+Ontario marginal tax rate at $95k income is 31%-32% or identifies bracket approximately $93k-$106k
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives a combined federal–Ontario marginal tax rate at $95,000 as “~29.65%,” and does not state a 31%–32% rate or identify the bracket as approximately $93k–$106k. Therefore it fails to provide the required marginal-rate range or bracket approximation.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Calculates $1,000 RRSP contribution saves approximately $310-$320 in tax at 31%-32% marginal rate
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response calculates RRSP tax savings using a 29.65% marginal rate (e.g., $1,000 saves ~$297) rather than stating that a $1,000 RRSP contribution saves approximately $310–$320 at a 31%–32% marginal rate. It does not include the required 31%–32% rate or the $310–$320 savings range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States 2025 RRSP annual dollar limit is $32,490
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not state that the 2025 RRSP annual dollar limit is $32,490. It instead mentions a different figure (“max $32,000 in 2026”) and never provides the required 2025 limit amount.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States RRSP contribution room is 18% of prior year earned income (lesser of percentage or dollar limit plus carry-forward)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states the RRSP contribution limit as “18% of previous year’s earned income” and notes there is also a maximum dollar cap and that the user should check CRA for precise room, which implies carry-forward/available room. This satisfies the requirement that RRSP room is the lesser of 18% or the dollar limit plus carry-forward.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Calculates 18% of $95,000 yields approximately $17,000-$17,100 in new RRSP contribution room
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions that RRSP contribution room is 18% of previous year’s earned income, but it never calculates 18% of $95,000 or states that this equals roughly $17,000–$17,100. The specific numeric result for new RRSP contribution room is missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States CESG provides 20% grant on first $2,500 contributed per child per year
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly explains CESG as “20% of the first $2,500/year/child” and notes this equals “up to $500/year,” which states the required grant structure.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States CESG annual maximum is $500 per child per year
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that CESG matches 20% of the first $2,500/year/child “up to $500/year,” which is the annual maximum per child.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States CESG lifetime maximum is $7,200 per child
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that the CESG has “a lifetime cap per child of $7,200” in the “How CESG Works” section.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains $5,000 contribution per child in one year can capture up to $1,000 CESG when unused grant room carried forward
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explains the CESG catch-up provision, stating that contributing up to $5,000 per child in a year can yield up to $1,000 of CESG when unused grant room is carried forward. This appears in the “Catch-up Provision” section and is reiterated in later allocation guidance.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States to receive CESG at ages 16-17 must have contributed either $2,000 before year child turned 15 OR $100 in any four prior years
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explains CESG basics and catch-up limits but does not state the specific eligibility rule for receiving CESG at ages 16–17 requiring either $2,000 contributed before the year the child turned 15 or $100 contributed in any four prior years. That exact condition is not mentioned anywhere in the RESP/CESG section.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States RESP lifetime contribution limit is $50,000 per beneficiary
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly mentions “the $50,000 RESP cap” when discussing a front-load strategy, which states the RESP lifetime contribution limit is $50,000 per beneficiary.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies $36,000 in contributions needed to maximize $7,200 CESG (since $7,200 divided by 20% equals $36,000)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states the CESG lifetime cap is $7,200 per child but never calculates or identifies that $36,000 in contributions are needed to maximize it (i.e., $7,200 ÷ 20% = $36,000). It discusses annual contribution amounts ($2,500/$5,000) and grant limits, but omits the specific $36,000 figure and the explicit division rationale.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes up to $14,000 additional contribution per child ($50,000 lifetime minus $36,000 for CESG) earns tax-deferred growth without additional grant
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions the $50,000 RESP cap and the $7,200 CESG lifetime maximum, but it does not note that up to $14,000 of additional contributions per child ($50,000 minus $36,000 needed to earn full CESG) can still be made for tax-deferred growth without additional grant. This specific $36,000/$14,000 framing is absent.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States contributor claims RRSP deduction immediately at their marginal rate whether contribution goes to own RRSP or spousal RRSP
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response says both individual and spousal RRSP contributions give the contributor a deduction now, but it does not explicitly state that the deduction is claimed immediately at the contributor’s marginal tax rate regardless of whether the contribution goes to their own RRSP or a spousal RRSP. It provides an example marginal rate (~29.65%) and tax savings, but doesn’t clearly tie that rate equivalence to both RRSP types as an immediate claim.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains spousal RRSP account is owned by spouse and withdrawals after attribution period are taxed to spouse not contributor
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response notes that in a spousal RRSP the contributor gets the deduction and that withdrawals are taxed to the spouse/partner after the three-year attribution rule, but it does not explain that the spousal RRSP account is owned by the spouse (annuitant) rather than the contributor. The ownership element is missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States spousal RRSP 3-calendar-year attribution rule (withdrawal in contribution year or following 2 calendar years attributed back to contributor)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions that spousal RRSP withdrawals are taxed to the spouse “if 3+ years pass” and refers generally to “attribution rules apply (<3 years),” but it does not state the specific 3-calendar-year attribution rule (withdrawals in the contribution year or the following two calendar years are attributed back to the contributor). The required explicit description of the contribution-year + next-two-years rule is missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes minimum RRIF payments from spousal account are exempt from attribution (only excess over minimum can be attributed back)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions spousal RRSP attribution rules generally (e.g., withdrawals taxed to spouse if 3+ years pass) but does not note the specific exception that minimum RRIF payments from a spousal account are exempt from attribution and only excess over the minimum is attributed back. This RRIF-minimum exemption is not addressed anywhere in the response.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains spousal RRSP creates income-splitting opportunity if spouse expected to be in lower tax bracket during retirement
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explains that a spousal RRSP lets the higher-earning spouse claim the deduction now while withdrawals are taxed to the lower-income spouse in retirement, reducing the household’s overall tax bill. It explicitly notes this is ideal when the partner is expected to have substantially lower taxable income in retirement, creating an income-splitting opportunity.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States 2025 TFSA annual contribution limit is $7,000
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states the TFSA annual limit as “(2026): $7,000,” but it does not state that the 2025 TFSA annual contribution limit is $7,000. The required element is an explicit statement about the 2025 TFSA limit being $7,000.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States TFSA withdrawals are tax-free and do not affect income-tested benefits
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that TFSA growth is tax-free and that withdrawals are not considered income and do not affect government benefits, which covers both tax-free withdrawals and no impact on income-tested benefits.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains withdrawn amount from TFSA is added back to contribution room on January 1 of following calendar year
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response notes that TFSA withdrawals “can be replaced the following calendar year,” but it does not explicitly state that the withdrawn amount is added back to TFSA contribution room on January 1 of the following calendar year. The specific timing (“January 1”) and the mechanism (“added back to contribution room”) are not clearly explained.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Calculates $2,500 per child equals $5,000 total annual RESP contribution for two children to maximize $1,000 combined annual CESG
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states the CESG is 20% of the first $2,500 per year per child and implies two children would contribute $2,500 each, but it never explicitly calculates $2,500 per child = $5,000 total annual RESP contribution to maximize a $1,000 combined annual CESG. breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains TFSA room restoration on January 1 enables withdrawal during slow months and recontribution when cash flow improves
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response notes that TFSA withdrawals can be used for an emergency fund and that withdrawn amounts can be recontributed the following calendar year, but it does not explain the January 1 room restoration as a strategy for withdrawing during slow months and recontributing when cash flow improves. breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response mentions that RRSP contribution room is “18% of previous year’s earned income” but does not explicitly connect the 2025 RRSP room to 2024 earned income or compute the approximate $17,100 figure from $95,000 × 18%. breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Recommends maximizing registered account contributions without addressing the stated 6-month emergency fund liquidity requirement

While it recommends maximizing RESP/CESG and contributing to RRSP/TFSA, it does so explicitly within the constraint of preserving emergency-fund liquidity.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses precise Canadian tax terms (RRSP RESP TFSA CESG RRIF attribution marginal rate) without generic language
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses multiple precise Canadian tax terms, including RRSP, spousal RRSP, RESP, TFSA, CESG, attribution rules, and marginal tax rate, rather than relying on generic language.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes multi-account strategy with headings numbered sections or tables for clarity
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is clearly organized with numbered sections (e.g., sections 1–9) and uses multiple tables (RESP comparison table and RRSP feature comparison table) to structure the multi-account strategy for clarity.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains professional financial planning tone without product sales marketing language or vague recommendations
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses a formal, advisory financial-planning style (headings, scenarios, tax discussion) and does not include product-pitch language or calls to buy specific products. Recommendations are generally specific (e.g., RESP $2,500/year/child, emergency fund target, sample monthly allocations) rather than purely vague.

presentation-quality.requirement: States a definitive recommended strategy for the RRSP-RESP-TFSA allocation question rather than only listing options
presentation-quality.explanation: The response provides a clear, prioritized sequence (emergency fund first, then RESP to maximize CESG, then RRSP, then TFSA) and gives a concrete sample monthly allocation across RESP/RRSP/TFSA. It also explicitly recommends when to use spousal RRSP versus individual RRSP rather than only listing possibilities.

presentation-quality.requirement: Explains fundamental concepts like what an RRSP or TFSA is to a financially sophisticated asker who already understands these accounts
presentation-quality.explanation: The response includes basic explanatory sections like “How CESG Works,” “How RRSPs Work,” and a TFSA annual limit/tax-advantage primer, which are fundamental definitions. These explanations are presented even though the query implies the asker already understands RRSP/TFSA/RESP mechanics, so the response exhibits the undesired behavior.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites canada.ca (Canada Revenue Agency) for RRSP RESP TFSA rules contribution limits and program details
citation-quality.explanation: The response includes a canada.ca/CRA citation for spousal RRSP contributions and a canada.ca citation for RESP limits, but it does not cite canada.ca (CRA) for TFSA rules/contribution limits and does not consistently use CRA canada.ca sources for RRSP/RESP/TFSA rules and program details as required.

citation-quality.requirement: References authoritative source for 2025 Ontario marginal tax rates (accounting firms provincial budget documents or established Canadian tax sites)
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites multiple sources, but none are authoritative references specifically for 2025 Ontario marginal tax rates (e.g., CRA, Ontario budget documents, major accounting firms, or established Canadian tax-rate sites). citation-quality.requirement: Cites authoritative Canadian tax resource for spousal RRSP attribution rules and timing (e.g. taxtips.ca major accounting firms or CRA publications)
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites an authoritative CRA publication on contributing to a spouse’s RRSP (Canada.ca CRA page) and discusses the spousal RRSP attribution timing rule (the 3-year rule). This satisfies the requirement to cite an authoritative Canadian tax resource for spousal RRSP attribution rules and timing.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites US tax authority sources (IRS.gov or US tax code provisions) as applicable to Canadian tax planning
